title: Glass Hour
description: C minor, 132 BPM, Ab to Eb to Cm (VI to III to i), 4-on-floor drums, and a short glassy stab. The choices are fully written for you, but the arrangement is staged like a polished garage record instead of a study loop.
what_youll_learn: How VI to III to i feels like bright, brighter, then home in C minor
How to let a stab do rhythmic work without turning into a full chord pad melody
How to keep the bass simple while the harmony carries the emotional shape
How to build a finished arrangement from a single 4-bar progression without losing the release-ready feel

steps.title: Set up the session
steps.instruction: Create a new Ableton Live set at 132 BPM and save it as 'original-07-glass-hour'. Make these tracks in order: DRUMS, BASS, PAD, STAB, and a return track named VERB. The whole track is about one motion: Ab major to Eb major to C minor.
steps.why: When the harmonic story is this simple, beginners can focus on hearing what the chord order feels like instead of getting lost in too many layers.
steps.tip: Say the roots out loud once: Ab, Eb, C. That descending path is the identity of the track.

steps.title: Load the drum samples
steps.instruction: Load a Drum Rack on DRUMS. Set C1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 1/KICKS/PH UKG1 ONESHOT Gum_Kick.wav', D1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 2/One Shots/Clap/PH_UKG2_Clap_19.wav', F#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 1/HATS/PH UKG1 ONESHOT Gum_ClosedHat.wav', G#1 = 'Production Hut- UK Garage Drums Volume 1/SHAKERS/PH UKG1 ONESHOT Cash_Shaker.wav'.
steps.why: The slightly rounder Gum kick suits this smoother harmonic movement, while the cleaner clap keeps the backbeat readable.
steps.tip: ClosedHat is the correct choice here. An open hat would make the track feel more aggressive than the harmony suggests.

steps.title: Program the drums
steps.instruction: Create a 2-bar DRUMS clip. All note lengths are 0.25 beats. KICK C1: 1.1.1 vel 95, 1.2.1 vel 87, 1.3.1 vel 93, 1.4.1 vel 85, 2.1.1 vel 95, 2.2.1 vel 88, 2.3.1 vel 94, 2.4.1 vel 86. CLAP D1: 1.2.1 vel 91, 1.4.1 vel 89, 2.2.1 vel 91, 2.4.1 vel 89. CLOSED HAT F#1: 1.1.3 vel 62, 1.2.3 vel 60, 1.3.3 vel 63, 1.4.3 vel 58, 2.1.3 vel 62, 2.2.3 vel 60, 2.3.3 vel 64, 2.4.3 vel 58. SHAKER G#1: 1.1.4 vel 42, 1.3.4 vel 40, 2.1.4 vel 42, 2.3.4 vel 40. Group DRUMS and add Glue Compressor with Ratio 4:1, Attack 3 ms, Release Auto, Threshold -5 dB, Soft Clip On.
steps.why: The quiet shaker on the fourth 16th of beats 1 and 3 adds a small push without sounding busy.
steps.tip: That shaker placement should feel like a small flick, not a second hat pattern.

steps.title: Write the bass roots
steps.instruction: On BASS load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Bass- Knocking Sub'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar MIDI clip with these notes: Ab1 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 90; Eb2 at 1.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72; Eb1 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 88; Bb1 at 2.4.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 70; C1 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 92; G1 at 4.4.1, length 0.5 beats, velocity 68.
steps.why: The final C minor home chord gets two bars because the track is supposed to feel like it has landed, not like it is rushing away again immediately.
steps.tip: The last G1 is only there to pull the loop back upward into Ab on bar 1.

steps.title: Process the bass
steps.instruction: After Serum on BASS add Saturator, Compressor, EQ Eight, and Utility. Saturator: Drive 4.2 dB, Soft Clip On, Output -2.5 dB. Compressor: Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -18 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.30 ms, Release 88 ms. EQ Eight: High-Pass 30 Hz at 24 dB slope. Utility: Width 0 percent. Set BASS fader to -5.0 dB.
steps.why: Nothing about the bass sound should distract from the harmonic motion. Clean, mono, and controlled is the whole job.
steps.tip: If the bass feels too buzzy, you changed the preset. Reload it and keep it stock.

steps.title: Load the pad and learn the chords
steps.instruction: On PAD load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Pad- Blind Date'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. The chords are Ab major = Ab, C, Eb; Eb major = Eb, G, Bb; C minor = C, Eb, G.
steps.why: The move from Ab to Eb to Cm works because each chord shares tones with the next one. That shared-note glue makes the progression feel expensive even though it is easy to program.
steps.tip: Notice how Eb appears in both Ab and Cm, and G appears in both Eb and Cm. Those overlaps are the smoothness.

steps.title: Program the pad and reverb
steps.instruction: Create a 4-bar PAD clip. Draw Ab3 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; C4 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; Eb4 at 1.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; Eb3 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 72; G3 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 68; Bb3 at 2.1.1, length 3.5 beats, velocity 64; C3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 72; Eb3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 68; G3 at 3.1.1, length 7.5 beats, velocity 64. On VERB add Reverb with Decay Time 2.6 s, Pre-Delay 11 ms, Dry/Wet 100 percent, then EQ Eight High-Pass 220 Hz. On PAD add Compressor with Sidechain On, Audio From DRUMS, Threshold -22 dB, Ratio 4:1, Attack 0.10 ms, Release 135 ms. Set PAD send to VERB at -18.0 dB and PAD fader to -12.0 dB.
steps.why: The pad is the emotional map of the track. The last 2 bars on C minor tell the ear that the phrase has truly arrived somewhere.
steps.tip: The pad should feel wider and slower than the stab. Let the two layers have different jobs.

steps.title: Add the stab rhythm
steps.instruction: On STAB load Serum 2 and open S1 Presets -> Garage -> 'Stab - Wide Load'. Leave the preset exactly as loaded. Create a 4-bar clip with these notes: Ab4 at 1.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 82; C5 at 1.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 76; Eb5 at 1.4.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 72; G4 at 2.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 80; Bb4 at 2.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; Eb5 at 2.4.3, length 0.25 beats, velocity 70; C5 at 3.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 82; G4 at 3.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 74; Eb5 at 4.1.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 80; G4 at 4.3.3, length 0.5 beats, velocity 72. Set STAB send to VERB at -21.0 dB and STAB fader to -14.0 dB.
steps.why: The stab hits after the beat instead of on the downbeat, which makes the groove feel like it is leaning forward instead of just announcing the chords.
steps.tip: Stab notes should be short and percussive. If they ring too long, shorten the MIDI notes before changing any sound settings.

steps.title: Create vocal space
steps.instruction: On PAD add EQ Eight after the Compressor: High-Pass 140 Hz, Bell 950 Hz at -3.5 dB with Q 1.50, Bell 2.4 kHz at -2.5 dB with Q 1.30. On STAB add EQ Eight: High-Pass 260 Hz, Bell 2.8 kHz at -2.0 dB with Q 1.30.
steps.why: This keeps the midrange focused. The stab is bright enough already and does not need extra voice-range weight.
steps.tip: The track should sound shiny, not harsh.

steps.title: Arrange the track
steps.instruction: Build the full arrangement exactly like this. Bars 1-8: intro, DRUMS only. Bars 9-16: drop 1A, DRUMS plus BASS plus PAD. Bars 17-24: drop 1B, add STAB. Bars 25-28: break A, PAD only. Bars 29-32: break B, PAD plus STAB, and automate the STAB send to VERB from -21 dB at bar 29 to -17 dB at bar 32. Delete all clips on bar 32 beat 4 for a 1-beat silence. Bars 33-48: drop 2A, all four tracks. Bars 49-64: drop 2B, keep all four tracks playing, automate the STAB track fader from -14.0 dB at bar 49 to -13.0 dB at bar 64, and automate the PAD send to VERB from -18 dB at bar 57 to -15 dB at bar 64. Bars 65-72: outro, mute STAB at bar 65, mute PAD at bar 69, mute BASS at bar 71, and let DRUMS finish bar 72.
steps.why: The track gets bigger through exact space and send changes, not by writing new parts. That keeps the harmonic story clean while still making the second half feel finished.
steps.tip: If bars 49-64 do not feel like the payoff, revisit the stab and pad automation before you touch the notes.

steps.title: Set the mix
steps.instruction: Set DRUMS group to -4.0 dB, BASS to -5.0 dB, PAD to -12.0 dB, STAB to -14.0 dB, VERB return to -19.0 dB, and Master to 0.0 dB. Add Limiter on Master with Ceiling -0.30 dB.
steps.why: These levels leave the drums and bass in charge while the harmonic layers stay clear and elegant.
steps.tip: If the stab pokes out too much, lower the track fader 1 dB before touching EQ.

steps.title: Export Glass Hour
steps.instruction: Export bars 1 through 72 as WAV, 24-bit, 44.1 kHz with filename 'original-07-glass-hour-v1.wav'. After export, listen to bars 49-64 and confirm the bright-to-home chord story is still obvious while the second drop feels wider and more finished than bars 17-24.
steps.why: If the chord story survives the bigger later section, the song is doing more than teaching theory. It is acting like a real record.
steps.tip: The most important feeling in this track is the arrival on C minor. If that feels weak, revisit pad balance before anything else.
